title: Can we teach generative artificial intelligence the design language of engineered living materials?
authors: Andrés Díaz Lantada, José A. Yáñez, William Solórzano-Requejo, Monsur Islam
abstract: This study presents a versatile ontology and a useful codification scheme for describing all kinds of engineered living materials (ELMs). The different components of the ontology, namely: families according to the taxonomy for ELMs, industrial applications and synthesis or processing methods, are systematically organized, enumerated, classified, codified and explained. The methodic application of the ontology to a set of 100 relevant examples of ELMs helps to demonstrate its utility and adaptability to many different types of ELMs with a wide range of industrial applications and obtained through numerous synthesis and processing methods. This proves that the developed ontology and codification schemes, with the glossary provided to support its implementation and application, can serve as a comprehensive classification tool for the emergent field of ELMs. Furthermore, the usability of the ELMs ontology and codification by a generative artificial intelligence (AI) is explored and validated by different means, checking that both natural language and the codification are understandable for describing ELMs, verifying that the generative AI adequately codifies examples of ELMs according to the ontology, and validating the synergic applicability of the ontology and codification with generative AI tools for illustrating novel ELMs and supporting their conceptual design. This study is expected to provide a universal language to facilitate communication in the ELMs field and to foster the discovery of new ELMs and related innovations, hoping it may accelerate scientific and technological discoveries.
summary: This paper introduces a new standardized design language for Engineered Living Materials (ELMs) using a hierarchical ontology and a three-letter codification scheme. By creating a 'genomic representation' for these biohybrid materials, the authors demonstrate that generative AI can be trained to understand, classify, and even visually illustrate novel material designs. The framework provides a way to bridge biological complexity with machine-readable data, potentially accelerating the discovery of new materials for applications ranging from tissue engineering to space exploration.
main_problem: The field of Engineered Living Materials (ELMs) lacks a standardized engineering design language and systematic classification tools to navigate its multidisciplinary complexity.
main_result: The authors developed a codified ontology and a 'genomic representation' for ELMs, demonstrating that generative AI can understand, classify, and use this language to illustrate novel material concepts.
method: The study uses a hierarchical taxonomy (inspired by Linnean taxonomy) combined with a three-letter codification scheme, validated through a dataset of 100 ELM examples and testing via generative AI (e.g., Microsoft Copilot) for text-to-image generation and design reasoning.
model_or_system: The system is a framework for Engineered Living Materials (ELMs), which are biohybrid materials where living cells (archaea, bacteria, eukaryotes, or synthetic) coexist with biomaterial scaffolds (polymers, ceramics, alloys, etc.).
key_observables: Not specified (focus is on ontological classification and AI-driven design generation).
important_parameters: Taxonomic ranks (Domain to Class), dimensionality (0D to 4D), material classes, and production methods.
main_assumptions: The study assumes that a structured linguistic framework can effectively bridge the gap between biological complexity and machine-readable data for AI-driven discovery.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows AI-generated illustrations of ELMs created from ontology prompts; Figure 2A presents a dendrogram of the ELM dataset; Figure 2B shows a bipartite knowledge graph of the ontology and ELM cases.
paper_structure: The paper identifies the lack of standardization, proposes a new ontology and codification scheme, applies it to a curated dataset of 100 materials, validates its utility using generative AI for visual and conceptual design, and explores its use in complex engineering tasks like space-habitat life support.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
